title: Building a Sampling Distribution of x̄

steps.instruction: The **Population** panel shows a strongly **right-skewed** population — most values sit low, with a few large values stretching a long tail to the right. Its true mean **μ** is marked. We can't see a whole population in real life, but here we can. We'll draw samples of **n = 10** and track the **mean x̄** of each sample.
steps.observe: Find **μ** marked on the Population panel. A single sample of 10 values will rarely have a mean exactly equal to μ — that sample-to-sample wobble is what we're about to study.

steps.instruction: **Before you draw anything, commit to a prediction.** Imagine taking *hundreds* of samples of n = 10 and plotting each sample's mean x̄.

gate.question: The population is right-skewed. Where will those sample means (x̄) pile up?

choices.text: Centered around μ, the population mean
choices.feedback: You've committed — now draw samples and watch where the center of the pile actually lands.

choices.text: Pulled to the right of μ, toward the long tail
choices.feedback: You've committed — the skew is tempting. Draw samples and check where the center really falls.
You've committed — draw and see whether the means spread out evenly or cluster somewhere.

steps.instruction: Click **+1** once. The **One sample** panel shows your 10 drawn values; they collapse to a single mean **x̄**, which drops as one dot into the **Sampling distribution** panel at the bottom. Click **+1** a few more times.

requires.hint: Click **+1** a few times (at least 5 draws), then continue.

steps.observe: Each dot is **one sample's mean** — not one observation. The x̄'s bounce around from sample to sample, but where do they seem to gather?

steps.instruction: Now click **+1000** to draw a thousand samples quickly and let the sampling distribution fill in.

requires.hint: Click **+1000** to draw at least 500 sample means, then answer below.

steps.observe: Look at the **Sampling distribution** panel and the **SD of x̄'s** readout, then compare it to the theory value **σ/√n**.

gate.question: Even though the population is strongly right-skewed, where is the distribution of x̄ centered, and how does its shape compare to the population?

choices.text: Centered at μ, and less skewed (more symmetric) than the population
Centered to the right of μ, and just as skewed as the population

gate.feedback: It centers at **μ**, and just averaging 10 values already pulls the shape toward symmetric — the skew is fading. Your **{{samples}}** sample means have an SD of x̄'s of **{{statSD}}**, close to the theory value σ/√n = **{{theorySE}}**. Watch the shape become fully bell-shaped when you increase n next.
gate.retryFeedback: Look at where the dots pile up relative to the μ line, and compare the shape of the bottom panel to the skewed Population panel above.

steps.instruction: Two different changes affect this picture in two different ways. First click **+1000 again** (more samples, same n = 10). Then click **Freeze this distribution to compare**, change **Sample size (n) to 100**, and draw **+1000** — so you see both at once.

requires.hint: Freeze the n = 10 distribution, set **Sample size (n)** to **100**, and draw **+1000**.

steps.observe: Compare the frozen n = 10 distribution to the new n = 100 one, and watch the **SD of x̄'s** readout drop.

gate.question: Which change made the sampling distribution **narrower** — and which just filled it in more smoothly?

choices.text: Increasing the sample size to n = 100 narrowed it

gate.feedback: Only a larger **n** narrows it. More samples (more dots) just fill in the *same* n = 10 distribution more smoothly — its spread doesn't change. A larger n produces a *different*, narrower distribution, because σ/√n shrinks as n grows. And notice the n = 100 distribution is now clearly bell-shaped — while the center stayed at μ the whole time.
gate.retryFeedback: Compare the frozen n = 10 distribution to the n = 100 one — same center at μ, different width. Then recall what adding more dots at n = 10 did to the width.

steps.instruction: You've built the sampling distribution of a sample mean from scratch — starting from a skewed population.
steps.reveal: The sample mean x̄ varies from sample to sample, but predictably: its distribution is **centered at the population mean μ**, has standard deviation **σ/√n**, and becomes approximately **normal** as n grows — even when the population itself is skewed. That last part is the **Central Limit Theorem**. Crucially, **more samples** sharpen the picture of that distribution, while a **larger n** changes it — shrinking the spread. This is the engine behind every confidence interval and t-test for a mean.
